Let \(N_r\) be the number of pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\) congruent to \(r \pmod 4\). We need

\[
N_0=N_1=N_2=N_3.
\]

Let

\[
c_r=\#\{i:a_i\equiv r\pmod 4\}.
\]

Then

\[
\begin{aligned}
N_0&=\binom{c_0}{2}+\binom{c_2}{2}+c_1c_3,\\
N_1&=c_0c_1+c_2c_3,\\
N_2&=c_0c_2+\binom{c_1}{2}+\binom{c_3}{2},\\
N_3&=c_0c_3+c_1c_2.
\end{aligned}
\]

Set

\[
E=c_0+c_2,\qquad O=c_1+c_3.
\]

Since \(N_0+N_2\) counts sums of same parity and \(N_1+N_3\) counts sums of opposite parity, equality gives

\[
\binom{E}{2}+\binom{O}{2}=EO.
\]

Thus

\[
(E-O)^2=E+O=n.
\]

So \(n\) must be a perfect square.

Now set

\[
u=c_0-c_2,\qquad v=c_1-c_3.
\]

From \(N_1=N_3\),

\[
(c_0-c_2)(c_1-c_3)=uv=0.
\]

From \(N_0=N_2\),

\[
u^2-v^2=E-O.
\]

Since \(uv=0\), this forces \(|E-O|\) to be a perfect square. But \((E-O)^2=n\), so \(n\) must be a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since \(7^4=2401>2025\).

It remains to show \(1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(c_0,c_1,c_2,c_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then the numbers of pairwise sums in each residue class are all

\[
209790.
\]

Finally, choose residues \(r_k\) with these counts and define

\[
a_k=8\cdot 2^k+r_k.
\]

Then \(a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_{1296}\), and if

\[
a_i+a_j=a_p+a_q,
\]

then

\[
8(2^i+2^j-2^p-2^q)
\]

has absolute value at most \(6\), so it must be \(0\). Hence \(\{i,j\}=\{p,q\}\), so all pairwise sums are distinct.

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1296}.
\]

Let the condition “each remainder appears the same number of times modulo \(4\)” refer to the pair sums \(a_i+a_j\).

Let

\[
c_r=\#\{i:a_i\equiv r\pmod 4\},\qquad r=0,1,2,3.
\]

We only need to study these residue counts first.

Define

\[
E=c_0+c_2,\qquad O=c_1+c_3,
\]

the numbers of even and odd terms. Pair sums with even residue come from two terms of the same parity, while pair sums with odd residue come from terms of opposite parity. Since all four residues appear equally often among pair sums,

\[
\binom E2+\binom O2=EO.
\]

Thus

\[
E(E-1)+O(O-1)=2EO
\]

so

\[
(E-O)^2=E+O=n.
\]

Therefore \(n\) must be a perfect square.

Now let

\[
d=c_0-c_2,\qquad e=c_1-c_3.
\]

Equality of residues \(1\) and \(3\) among pair sums gives

\[
c_0c_1+c_2c_3=c_0c_3+c_1c_2,
\]

so

\[
(c_0-c_2)(c_1-c_3)=de=0.
\]

Equality of residues \(0\) and \(2\) gives

\[
d^2-e^2=E-O.
\]

Since \(de=0\), one of \(d,e\) is zero, so \(|E-O|\) must itself be a perfect square. But \(n=(E-O)^2\), hence \(n\) must be a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since \(7^4=2401>2025\).

It remains to show \(1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(c_0,c_1,c_2,c_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then \(E=666,\ O=630\), so \(E-O=36=6^2\), and one checks that the four residue classes of pair sums each occur

\[
\frac{\binom{1296}{2}}4=209790
\]

times.

Finally, choose any list of \(1296\) residues with these counts, say \(r_i\in\{0,1,2,3\}\), and set

\[
a_i=4\cdot 10^i+r_i.
\]

Then \(a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_{1296}\), and all pair sums are distinct because the dominant powers of \(10\) make \(10^i+10^j\) unique for \(i<j\).

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1296}.
\]

Let the condition “each remainder appears the same number of times modulo \(4\)” refer to the pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\).

For each residue class modulo \(4\), let

\[
c_r=\#\{i:a_i\equiv r\pmod 4\},\qquad r=0,1,2,3.
\]

Let \(N_t\) be the number of pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\) congruent to \(t\pmod 4\). We need

\[
N_0=N_1=N_2=N_3.
\]

Use the fourth root of unity \(i\). Define

\[
P=\sum_{r=0}^3 c_r i^r,\qquad Q=\sum_{r=0}^3 c_r(-1)^r.
\]

Since the sums are equally distributed modulo \(4\),

\[
\sum_{t=0}^3 N_t i^t=0.
\]

But

\[
\sum_{t=0}^3 N_t i^t
=\sum_{p<q} i^{a_p+a_q}
=\frac{\left(\sum_p i^{a_p}\right)^2-\sum_p i^{2a_p}}2
=\frac{P^2-Q}{2}.
\]

Hence

\[
P^2=Q.
\]

Similarly, using \((-1)^t\),

\[
0=\sum_{t=0}^3 N_t(-1)^t
=\frac{Q^2-n}{2},
\]

so

\[
Q^2=n.
\]

Now \(P\) is a Gaussian integer. Write \(P=x+yi\). Since \(P^2=Q\) is real,

\[
2xy=0.
\]

Thus either \(P=x\) or \(P=yi\), and in either case \(|Q|\) is a perfect square. Therefore

\[
n=Q^2
\]

must be a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since \(7^4=2401>2025\). So \(n\le 1296\).

It remains to show \(n=1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(c_0,c_1,c_2,c_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then

\[
P=336+315i-330-315i=6,
\]

and

\[
Q=336-315+330-315=36.
\]

Thus

\[
P^2=36=Q,\qquad Q^2=1296,
\]

so the pairwise sums are equally distributed modulo \(4\).

Finally, we can choose actual positive integers with these residues and with all pairwise sums distinct by selecting them recursively: at each step choose a sufficiently large integer in the desired residue class modulo \(4\), avoiding the finitely many values that would create a repeated pairwise sum.

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1296}
\]

Let the common condition “each remainder modulo 4 appears the same number of times” refer to the pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\).

Suppose such a sequence of length \(n\) exists. Let

\[
x_r=\#\{i:a_i\equiv r\pmod 4\},\qquad r=0,1,2,3.
\]

Let \(T\) be the common number of unordered pair sums in each residue class modulo \(4\).

Now count ordered pairs \((i,j)\), allowing \(i=j\), whose sum is congruent to each residue modulo \(4\). Let these counts be \(M_0,M_1,M_2,M_3\). Since unordered distinct pairs contribute twice, and diagonal pairs \(i=j\) contribute residue \(0\) if \(a_i\) is even and residue \(2\) if \(a_i\) is odd, we get

\[
M_0=2T+x_0+x_2,\qquad M_1=2T,
\]
\[
M_2=2T+x_1+x_3,\qquad M_3=2T.
\]

Set

\[
A=x_0+x_2,\qquad B=x_1+x_3.
\]

Then \(A+B=n\). Also,

\[
M_0-M_1+M_2-M_3=A+B=n.
\]

But the same quantity equals

\[
\sum_{i,j}(-1)^{a_i+a_j}
=\left(\sum_i(-1)^{a_i}\right)^2
=(A-B)^2.
\]

Hence

\[
n=(A-B)^2.
\]

So \(n\) must be a perfect square.

Next consider modulo \(4\) using \(i=\sqrt{-1}\). We have

\[
M_0-M_2+i(M_1-M_3)=A-B.
\]

But also

\[
M_0-M_2+i(M_1-M_3)
=\sum_{p,q} i^{a_p+a_q}
=\left(\sum_p i^{a_p}\right)^2.
\]

Now

\[
\sum_p i^{a_p}=(x_0-x_2)+i(x_1-x_3).
\]

Let

\[
u=x_0-x_2,\qquad v=x_1-x_3.
\]

Then

\[
(u+iv)^2=A-B.
\]

Since the right-hand side is real, we need \(uv=0\). Therefore \(|A-B|\) itself must be a perfect square. Since

\[
n=(A-B)^2,
\]

it follows that \(n\) must be a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since \(7^4=2401>2025\). So \(n\le 1296\).

It remains to show \(n=1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(x_0,x_1,x_2,x_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then the numbers of pair sums in residues \(0,1,2,3\) are respectively

\[
\binom{336}{2}+\binom{330}{2}+315^2=209790,
\]

\[
336\cdot315+330\cdot315=209790,
\]

\[
336\cdot330+2\binom{315}{2}=209790,
\]

\[
336\cdot315+315\cdot330=209790.
\]

So the four residue classes occur equally often.

Finally, choose residues \(r_i\in\{0,1,2,3\}\) with exactly those counts and define

\[
a_i=4\cdot 10^i+r_i.
\]

Then \(a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_{1296}\). Also, if

\[
a_i+a_j=a_k+a_\ell,
\]

then

\[
4(10^i+10^j-10^k-10^\ell)=(r_k+r_\ell)-(r_i+r_j),
\]

whose right-hand side has absolute value at most \(6\). The left-hand side is a multiple of \(40\), so it must be \(0\). Hence

\[
10^i+10^j=10^k+10^\ell,
\]

forcing \(\{i,j\}=\{k,\ell\}\). Thus all pairwise sums are distinct.

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1296}.
\]

Let the modulo condition refer to the pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\).

Suppose the number of terms congruent to \(r \pmod 4\) is \(m_r\), for \(r=0,1,2,3\). We want the pairwise sums to be equally distributed among the four residues modulo \(4\).

Define

\[
U=\sum_{i=1}^n i^{a_i}, \qquad V=\sum_{i=1}^n (-1)^{a_i}.
\]

If the pairwise sums are equally distributed modulo \(4\), then

\[
\sum_{1\le i<j\le n} i^{a_i+a_j}=0,
\]

so

\[
0=\frac{U^2-\sum_i i^{2a_i}}2=\frac{U^2-V}{2}.
\]

Thus

\[
U^2=V.
\]

Also,

\[
\sum_{1\le i<j\le n} (-1)^{a_i+a_j}=0,
\]

so

\[
0=\frac{V^2-n}{2},
\]

hence

\[
V^2=n.
\]

Now write

\[
U=(m_0-m_2)+i(m_1-m_3)=A+iB.
\]

Since \(U^2=V\) is real, we need

\[
2AB=0,
\]

so \(A=0\) or \(B=0\). Therefore

\[
n=V^2=(A^2-B^2)^2,
\]

which is a fourth power. Hence \(n\) must be a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since

\[
7^4=2401>2025.
\]

It remains to show \(n=1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(m_0,m_1,m_2,m_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then the numbers of pairwise sums congruent to \(0,1,2,3\pmod 4\) are respectively

\[
\binom{336}{2}+\binom{330}{2}+315^2=209790,
\]

\[
336\cdot 315+330\cdot 315=209790,
\]

\[
336\cdot 330+2\binom{315}{2}=209790,
\]

\[
336\cdot 315+315\cdot 330=209790.
\]

So the pairwise sums are equally distributed modulo \(4\).

Finally, we can choose actual positive integers with these residues recursively, each new term much larger than all previous ones, ensuring all pairwise sums are distinct.

Therefore the largest possible value is

\[
\boxed{1296}
\]

Let \(N_r\) be the number of pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\) congruent to \(r \pmod 4\). We need

\[
N_0=N_1=N_2=N_3.
\]

Let

\[
x_r=\#\{i:a_i\equiv r\pmod 4\},\qquad r=0,1,2,3.
\]

Then

\[
x_0+x_1+x_2+x_3=n.
\]

The numbers of pairwise sums in each residue class are

\[
\begin{aligned}
N_0&=\binom{x_0}{2}+\binom{x_2}{2}+x_1x_3,\\
N_1&=x_0x_1+x_2x_3,\\
N_2&=x_0x_2+\binom{x_1}{2}+\binom{x_3}{2},\\
N_3&=x_0x_3+x_1x_2.
\end{aligned}
\]

Let

\[
e=x_0+x_2,\qquad o=x_1+x_3.
\]

Since \(N_0+N_2\) counts pairs with even sum,

\[
N_0+N_2=\binom e2+\binom o2.
\]

But if all four \(N_r\) are equal, then

\[
N_0+N_2=\frac12\binom n2.
\]

Thus

\[
\binom e2+\binom o2=\frac12\binom n2.
\]

Since \(e+o=n\), this simplifies to

\[
(e-o)^2=n.
\]

So \(n\) must be a perfect square.

Now set

\[
d=x_0-x_2,\qquad f=x_1-x_3.
\]

From \(N_1=N_3\),

\[
(x_0-x_2)(x_1-x_3)=df=0.
\]

Also, from \(N_0=N_2\),

\[
d^2-f^2=e-o.
\]

Since \(df=0\), one of \(d,f\) is zero, so \(|e-o|\) must be a perfect square. But \(|e-o|=\sqrt n\). Therefore \(\sqrt n\) is a perfect square, meaning \(n\) is a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since \(7^4=2401>2025\).

It remains to show \(n=1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(x_0,x_1,x_2,x_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then

\[
\begin{aligned}
N_0&=\binom{336}{2}+\binom{330}{2}+315^2=209790,\\
N_1&=336\cdot315+330\cdot315=209790,\\
N_2&=336\cdot330+2\binom{315}{2}=209790,\\
N_3&=336\cdot315+330\cdot315=209790.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the residue classes of the pairwise sums are equally distributed.

Finally, choose residues \(r_i\in\{0,1,2,3\}\) with those counts and define

\[
a_i=4\cdot 10^i+r_i.
\]

Then \(a_1<a_2<\cdots<a_{1296}\), and all pairwise sums are distinct because the dominant terms \(10^i+10^j\) uniquely determine the pair \(\{i,j\}\).

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1296}.
\]

Let \(N_r\) be the number of pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\) congruent to \(r \pmod 4\). We need

\[
N_0=N_1=N_2=N_3.
\]

Let \(b_r\) be the number of terms \(a_i\equiv r\pmod 4\). Then

\[
b_0+b_1+b_2+b_3=n.
\]

The pair-sum residue counts are

\[
\begin{aligned}
N_0&=\binom{b_0}{2}+\binom{b_2}{2}+b_1b_3,\\
N_1&=b_0b_1+b_2b_3,\\
N_2&=b_0b_2+\binom{b_1}{2}+\binom{b_3}{2},\\
N_3&=b_0b_3+b_1b_2.
\end{aligned}
\]

Set

\[
A=b_0+b_2,\qquad B=b_1+b_3.
\]

Since \(N_0+N_2=N_1+N_3\), we get

\[
\binom{A}{2}+\binom{B}{2}=AB.
\]

Thus

\[
(A-B)^2=A+B=n.
\]

So \(n\) must be a perfect square.

Now let

\[
u=b_0-b_2,\qquad v=b_1-b_3.
\]

From \(N_1=N_3\),

\[
(b_0-b_2)(b_1-b_3)=uv=0.
\]

Also, from \(N_0=N_2\),

\[
u^2-v^2=A-B.
\]

Since \(uv=0\), either \(u=0\) or \(v=0\), so \(A-B\) must itself be a square or the negative of a square. Therefore \(|A-B|\) is a square, and since

\[
n=(A-B)^2,
\]

\(n\) must be a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since \(7^4=2401>2025\).

It remains to show \(1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(b_0,b_1,b_2,b_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then one checks

\[
N_0=N_1=N_2=N_3=209790.
\]

Finally, choose residues \(r_i\) with these counts and define

\[
a_i=8\cdot 2^i+r_i.
\]

Then \(a_i\equiv r_i\pmod 4\), and the sequence is strictly increasing. Also, if

\[
a_i+a_j=a_k+a_\ell,
\]

then

\[
8(2^i+2^j-2^k-2^\ell)=(r_k+r_\ell)-(r_i+r_j).
\]

The right side has absolute value at most \(6\), while the left side is divisible by \(8\), so both sides are \(0\). Hence

\[
2^i+2^j=2^k+2^\ell,
\]

forcing \(\{i,j\}=\{k,\ell\}\). Thus all pairwise sums are distinct.

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1296}
\]

Let the condition “each remainder appears the same number of times modulo \(4\)” refer to the pairwise sums \(a_i+a_j\).

Let \(c_r\) be the number of terms congruent to \(r \pmod 4\), for \(r=0,1,2,3\).

Suppose the pairwise sums are equally distributed modulo \(4\).

Define

\[
E=c_0+c_2,\qquad O=c_1+c_3,
\]

the numbers of even and odd terms. Since the sums are equally distributed modulo \(4\), in particular equally many sums are even and odd, so

\[
\binom E2+\binom O2=EO.
\]

Thus

\[
\frac{E(E-1)+O(O-1)}2=EO
\]

which simplifies to

\[
(E-O)^2=E+O=n.
\]

So \(n\) must be a perfect square.

Now use fourth roots of unity. Let

\[
z=c_0-c_2+i(c_1-c_3).
\]

Equidistribution modulo \(4\) implies

\[
\sum_{p<q} i^{a_p+a_q}=0.
\]

But

\[
\sum_{p<q} i^{a_p+a_q}
=\frac{z^2-\sum_p i^{2a_p}}2
=\frac{z^2-(E-O)}2.
\]

Hence

\[
z^2=E-O.
\]

Writing \(z=x+iy\), where

\[
x=c_0-c_2,\qquad y=c_1-c_3,
\]

we get

\[
(x+iy)^2=E-O.
\]

The right side is real, so \(xy=0\). Therefore \(|E-O|\) must itself be a square. Since

\[
n=(E-O)^2,
\]

we conclude that \(n\) must be a fourth power.

The largest fourth power not exceeding \(2025\) is

\[
6^4=1296,
\]

since \(7^4=2401>2025\).

It remains to show \(n=1296\) is attainable. Take residue counts

\[
(c_0,c_1,c_2,c_3)=(336,315,330,315).
\]

Then the numbers of pairwise sums in each residue class are:

\[
\begin{aligned}
0 &: \binom{336}{2}+\binom{330}{2}+315^2=209790,\\
1 &: 336\cdot315+330\cdot315=209790,\\
2 &: 336\cdot330+2\binom{315}{2}=209790,\\
3 &: 336\cdot315+315\cdot330=209790.
\end{aligned}
\]

So the sums are equally distributed modulo \(4\).

Finally, we can choose actual positive integers with these prescribed residues and with all pairwise sums distinct by a standard greedy construction: choose each next term in its required residue class, larger than all previous terms, avoiding the finitely many values that would create a repeated pairwise sum.

Therefore the largest possible \(n\) is

\[
\boxed{1296}
\]

Let the four colors be represented by the elements of \(\mathbb F_2^2\):

\[
A,B,C,D \leftrightarrow 00,01,10,11.
\]

In \(\mathbb F_2^2\), the sum of all four colors is \(0\). So if a \(2\times 2\) square contains all four colors, then its four entries have XOR-sum \(0\).

Let the grid entries be \(x_{ij}\). Then every adjacent \(2\times2\) square satisfies

\[
x_{ij}+x_{i+1,j}+x_{i,j+1}+x_{i+1,j+1}=0.
\]

Thus the whole grid has the form

\[
x_{ij}=r_i+c_j
\]

for some row sequence \(r_i\) and column sequence \(c_j\) in \(\mathbb F_2^2\).

Now define the vertical differences

\[
v_i=r_i+r_{i+1}
\]

and horizontal differences

\[
h_j=c_j+c_{j+1}.
\]

For a \(2\times2\) square to contain all four colors, we need

\[
v_i\neq 0,\qquad h_j\neq 0,\qquad v_i\neq h_j.
\]

There are only \(3\) nonzero elements in \(\mathbb F_2^2\). Since the grid is \(2022\times2022\), there are

\[
2021
\]

vertical differences and \(2021\) horizontal differences.

So we need two length-\(2021\) words over a \(3\)-letter alphabet such that no letter appears in both words.

Count them by the set of letters used by the vertical word:

- If it uses exactly \(1\) letter: \(3\) choices, and the horizontal word can use either of the remaining \(2\) letters freely, giving \(2^{2021}\) choices.
- If it uses exactly \(2\) letters: \(3\) choices for the pair, and the vertical word must use both, giving \(2^{2021}-2\) choices. The horizontal word is then forced to use only the remaining letter.

Therefore the number of valid difference-pairs is

\[
3\cdot 2^{2021}+3(2^{2021}-2)=6(2^{2021}-1).
\]

Finally, choosing the top-left color gives \(4\) choices, and then the whole grid is determined. Hence the number of perfect grids is

\[
4\cdot 6(2^{2021}-1)=24(2^{2021}-1).
\]

\[
\boxed{24(2^{2021}-1)}
\]

Assign the four colors to the elements of \(\mathbb Z_2^2\):

\[
A,B,C,D \leftrightarrow 00,01,10,11.
\]

In any \(2\times 2\) square containing all four colors, the xor-sum of the four entries is \(0\). Thus for entries \(x_{i,j}\),

\[
x_{i,j}+x_{i+1,j}+x_{i,j+1}+x_{i+1,j+1}=0.
\]

So horizontal differences are constant down columns, and vertical differences are constant across rows. Let

\[
p_i=x_{i,j}+x_{i+1,j},\qquad q_j=x_{i,j}+x_{i,j+1}.
\]

For a \(2\times 2\) square to contain all four colors, \(p_i\) and \(q_j\) must be distinct nonzero elements of \(\mathbb Z_2^2\). There are exactly \(3\) nonzero elements total.

Here there are \(2021\) vertical increments \(p_i\) and \(2021\) horizontal increments \(q_j\). Their used sets must be disjoint subsets of the \(3\) nonzero elements.

Let \(L=2021\). Count ordered pairs of increment sequences \((p,q)\):

- both use exactly one symbol: \(3\cdot 2=6\),
- \(p\) uses one symbol and \(q\) uses the other two: \(3(2^L-2)\),
- \(q\) uses one symbol and \(p\) uses the other two: \(3(2^L-2)\).

Thus the number of valid increment pairs is

\[
6+6(2^L-2)=6(2^L-1).
\]

Finally, choose the top-left cell color in \(4\) ways. Therefore the total number of perfect grids is

\[
4\cdot 6(2^{2021}-1)=24(2^{2021}-1).
\]

\[
\boxed{24(2^{2021}-1)}
\]

Label the four colors as the elements of \(\mathbb F_2^2\):

\[
A=00,\quad B=01,\quad C=10,\quad D=11.
\]

In \(\mathbb F_2^2\), the sum of all four elements is \(0\). Therefore, if every \(2\times 2\) square contains all four colors, then for every adjacent block

\[
\begin{matrix}
x_{i,j} & x_{i,j+1}\\
x_{i+1,j} & x_{i+1,j+1}
\end{matrix}
\]

we have

\[
x_{i,j}+x_{i,j+1}+x_{i+1,j}+x_{i+1,j+1}=0.
\]

So

\[
x_{i+1,j+1}=x_{i,j}+x_{i,j+1}+x_{i+1,j}.
\]

Thus the whole grid is determined by the first cell, the row increments, and the column increments.

Let

\[
R_i=x_{i+1,1}+x_{i,1},\qquad C_j=x_{1,j+1}+x_{1,j}.
\]

There are \(2021\) row increments and \(2021\) column increments.

For a \(2\times 2\) square to contain all four colors, we need

\[
R_i\neq 0,\qquad C_j\neq 0,\qquad R_i\neq C_j.
\]

There are exactly \(3\) nonzero elements in \(\mathbb F_2^2\). So we must choose two sequences

\[
(R_1,\dots,R_{2021}),\qquad (C_1,\dots,C_{2021})
\]

from a \(3\)-element set such that no value appears in both sequences.

Let \(L=2021\).

Count the possibilities for the \(R_i\)'s:

- If the \(R_i\)'s use exactly \(1\) value: \(3\) choices, and then each \(C_j\) has \(2\) choices, giving  
  \[
  3\cdot 2^L.
  \]

- If the \(R_i\)'s use exactly \(2\) values: choose those values in \(3\) ways, and the sequence must use both, giving \(2^L-2\) possibilities. Then every \(C_j\) is forced to be the remaining value. This gives  
  \[
  3(2^L-2).
  \]

So the number of valid increment choices is

\[
3\cdot 2^L+3(2^L-2)=6(2^L-1).
\]

Finally, the top-left cell can be chosen in \(4\) ways, so the total number of grids is

\[
4\cdot 6(2^{2021}-1)=24(2^{2021}-1).

\boxed{24(2^{2021}-1)}

\boxed{24(2^{2021}-1)}

3\cdot 2^L
\]
choices.

If \(v\) uses exactly 2 symbols:  
\[
3(2^L-2)
\]
choices, since \(h\) must use the remaining symbol.

So the number of possible difference pairs is

\[
3\cdot 2^L+3(2^L-2)=6(2^L-1).
\]

Finally, \(x_{1,1}\) can be chosen in \(4\) ways, giving

\[
4\cdot 6(2^{2021}-1)=24(2^{2021}-1).
\]

\[
\boxed{24(2^{2021}-1)}
\]

Assign the four colors to the elements of \(\mathbb{Z}_2^2\):

\[
A=00,\quad B=01,\quad C=10,\quad D=11.
\]

For a cell color \(x_{i,j}\), define horizontal and vertical differences:

\[
h_{i,j}=x_{i,j}+x_{i,j+1},\qquad v_{i,j}=x_{i,j}+x_{i+1,j}.
\]

In any \(2\times 2\) square containing all four colors, the two horizontal differences are equal, and the two vertical differences are equal. Also, the horizontal and vertical differences must be distinct nonzero elements of \(\mathbb{Z}_2^2\).

So for the whole grid:

- each column-gap has a fixed horizontal difference \(a_j\),
- each row-gap has a fixed vertical difference \(b_i\),
- and we need \(a_j\neq b_i\) for every \(i,j\).

There are \(2021\) horizontal gaps and \(2021\) vertical gaps. Each \(a_j,b_i\) is one of the \(3\) nonzero elements of \(\mathbb{Z}_2^2\).

Let \(L=2021\). We count pairs of sequences

\[
(a_1,\dots,a_L),\qquad (b_1,\dots,b_L)
\]

over a \(3\)-element alphabet such that the two sequences share no symbol.

Cases:

1. The \(a\)-sequence uses exactly \(1\) symbol.  
   There are \(3\) choices for it, and then the \(b\)-sequence can use any of the remaining \(2\) symbols:

   \[
   3\cdot 2^L.
   \]

2. The \(a\)-sequence uses exactly \(2\) symbols.  
   There are \(3\) choices for those two symbols, and the sequence must use both, giving \(2^L-2\) possibilities. Then the \(b\)-sequence is forced to use only the remaining symbol:

   \[
   3(2^L-2).
   \]

Thus the number of possible difference patterns is

\[
3\cdot 2^L+3(2^L-2)=6(2^L-1).
\]

Finally, the top-left cell can be any of the \(4\) colors, and then the entire grid is determined. Therefore the number of perfect grids is

\[
4\cdot 6(2^{2021}-1)=24(2^{2021}-1).
\]

\[
\boxed{24(2^{2021}-1)}
\]

Let the 2020 points be \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad 0\le x_i,y_i\le 1.
\]

So \(y_i=1-x_i\).

We need the smallest integer \(k\) such that we can always split the points into two groups \(A,B\) with

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le k,\qquad \sum_{i\in B} x_i\le k.
\]

---

### 1. Show that \(k=506\) always works

Sort the \(x_i\)'s in decreasing order:

\[
x_1\ge x_2\ge \cdots \ge x_{2020}.
\]

Let

\[
S=\sum_{i=1}^{2020}x_i,\qquad m=\lfloor S\rfloor.
\]

Put the first \(m\) points into group \(A\), and the remaining points into group \(B\). Let

\[
P_m=\sum_{i=1}^m x_i.
\]

Then

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i=\sum_{i=1}^m(1-x_i)=m-P_m,
\]

and

\[
\sum_{i\in B}x_i=S-P_m.
\]

Since \(m\le S\),

\[
m-P_m\le S-P_m.
\]

So it is enough to bound \(S-P_m\).

Because \(P_m\) is the sum of the \(m\) largest \(x_i\)'s,

\[
P_m\ge \frac{mS}{2020}.
\]

Thus

\[
S-P_m\le S\left(1-\frac m{2020}\right).
\]

Also \(S<m+1\), so

\[
S-P_m<(m+1)\left(1-\frac m{2020}\right)
=\frac{(m+1)(2020-m)}{2020}.
\]

The product \((m+1)(2020-m)\) is maximized when the factors are as close as possible, i.e. \(1010\) and \(1011\). Therefore

\[
S-P_m\le \frac{1010\cdot 1011}{2020}=505.5<506.
\]

Hence both required sums are always \(<506\), so \(k=506\) works.

---

### 2. Show that \(k=505\) does not always work

Take all 2020 points to coincide at

\[
\left(\frac{2021}{4040},\frac{2019}{4040}\right).
\]

Suppose \(r\) points are placed in the group whose \(y\)-sum must be at most \(505\). Then we would need

\[
r\cdot \frac{2019}{4040}\le 505,
\]

which implies

\[
r\le 1010.
\]

The other group has \(2020-r\) points, and its \(x\)-sum would need to satisfy

\[
(2020-r)\cdot \frac{2021}{4040}\le 505,
\]

which implies

\[
2020-r\le 1009,
\]

so

\[
r\ge 1011.
\]

This is impossible. Therefore \(k=505\) fails.

Thus the smallest possible integer is

\[
\boxed{506}.
\]

Let the 2020 points be \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad 0\le x_i,y_i\le 1.
\]

We need the smallest integer \(k\) such that we can always partition the points into two groups \(A,B\) with

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le k,\qquad \sum_{i\in B} x_i\le k.
\]

We will prove the answer is \(506\).

---

### 1. Prove \(k=506\) always works

Order the points so that

\[
x_1\ge x_2\ge \cdots \ge x_{2020}.
\]

Then

\[
y_1\le y_2\le \cdots \le y_{2020}.
\]

For \(m=0,1,\dots,2020\), put the first \(m\) points into group \(A\), and the remaining points into group \(B\). Define

\[
P_m=\sum_{i=1}^m y_i,\qquad Q_m=\sum_{i=m+1}^{2020} x_i.
\]

We want some \(m\) such that

\[
P_m\le 506,\qquad Q_m\le 506.
\]

Suppose no such \(m\) exists. Let \(r\) be the largest integer with

\[
P_r\le 506.
\]

Then necessarily

\[
P_{r+1}>506,\qquad Q_r>506.
\]

Let \(a=y_{r+1}\). Since \(y_1,\dots,y_{r+1}\le a\),

\[
P_{r+1}\le (r+1)a.
\]

So

\[
(r+1)a>506.
\]

Also \(x_{r+1}=1-a\), and since \(x_{r+1},\dots,x_{2020}\le 1-a\),

\[
Q_r\le (2020-r)(1-a).
\]

Thus

\[
(2020-r)(1-a)>506.
\]

Therefore

\[
a>\frac{506}{r+1},\qquad 1-a>\frac{506}{2020-r}.
\]

Adding,

\[
1>506\left(\frac1{r+1}+\frac1{2020-r}\right).
\]

But

\[
(r+1)+(2020-r)=2021,
\]

so by AM-HM,

\[
\frac1{r+1}+\frac1{2020-r}\ge \frac4{2021}.
\]

Hence

\[
506\left(\frac1{r+1}+\frac1{2020-r}\right)\ge \frac{2024}{2021}>1,
\]

a contradiction. Therefore \(k=506\) always works.

---

### 2. Prove \(k=505\) does not always work

Take all 2020 points to coincide at

\[
\left(\frac{2021}{4040},\frac{2019}{4040}\right).
\]

Suppose \(m\) points are placed in group \(A\), whose \(y\)-sum must be at most \(505\). Then group \(B\) has \(2020-m\) points.

If \(m\le 1010\), then

\[
\sum_{i\in B}x_i\ge 1010\cdot \frac{2021}{4040}=\frac{2021}{4}>505.
\]

If \(m\ge 1011\), then

\[
\sum_{i\in A}y_i\ge 1011\cdot \frac{2019}{4040}>505.
\]

So no partition works for \(k=505\). Therefore the smallest positive integer is

\[
\boxed{506}
\]

Let the 2020 points be \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad 0\le x_i,y_i\le 1.
\]

We will show the smallest integer is \(506\).

### 1. Prove \(506\) always works

Sort the points so that

\[
x_1\le x_2\le \cdots \le x_{2020}.
\]

For \(m=0,1,\dots,2020\), define

\[
P_m=\sum_{i=1}^m x_i,\qquad Q_m=\sum_{i=m+1}^{2020} y_i.
\]

If we put the first \(m\) points into the group whose \(x\)-coordinate sum is controlled, and the remaining points into the group whose \(y\)-coordinate sum is controlled, we need

\[
P_m\le 506,\qquad Q_m\le 506.
\]

We claim such an \(m\) always exists.

Suppose not. Let \(m\) be the largest integer such that \(P_m\le 506\). Then \(m<2020\), and since no \(m\) works, we must have

\[
Q_m>506.
\]

Also, by maximality,

\[
P_{m+1}>506.
\]

Let \(a=x_{m+1}\). Since \(x_1,\dots,x_{m+1}\le a\),

\[
P_{m+1}\le (m+1)a,
\]

so

\[
(m+1)a>506.
\]

Similarly, since \(x_{m+1},\dots,x_{2020}\ge a\), we have \(y_i=1-x_i\le 1-a\), so

\[
Q_m\le (2020-m)(1-a).
\]

But \(Q_m>506\), hence

\[
(2020-m)(1-a)>506.
\]

Therefore,

\[
a>\frac{506}{m+1},\qquad 1-a>\frac{506}{2020-m}.
\]

Adding,

\[
1>506\left(\frac1{m+1}+\frac1{2020-m}\right).
\]

Thus

\[
(m+1)(2020-m)>506\cdot 2021.
\]

But the maximum possible value of \((m+1)(2020-m)\) is

\[
1010\cdot 1011=1\,021\,110,
\]

while

\[
506\cdot 2021=1\,022\,626,
\]

a contradiction. Hence some \(m\) works, so \(506\) always suffices.

### 2. Show \(505\) does not always work

Take all 2020 points equal to

\[
\left(\frac12-\varepsilon,\frac12+\varepsilon\right),
\]

where \(0<\varepsilon<\frac1{2022}\).

Suppose \(505\) worked. Let the group whose \(x\)-coordinate sum is bounded contain \(m\) points. Then

\[
m\left(\frac12-\varepsilon\right)\le 505.
\]

Since \(\varepsilon<\frac1{2022}\),

\[
\frac12-\varepsilon>\frac{505}{1011},
\]

so

\[
m<1011,
\]

hence

\[
m\le 1010.
\]

The other group has \(2020-m\) points, and its \(y\)-coordinate sum must be at most \(505\), so

\[
(2020-m)\left(\frac12+\varepsilon\right)\le 505.
\]

But \(\frac12+\varepsilon>\frac12\), so

\[
2020-m<1010,
\]

hence

\[
m\ge 1011.
\]

Contradiction. Therefore \(505\) is not enough.

So the smallest positive integer is

\[
\boxed{506}
\]

Let the 2020 points be \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad 0\le x_i,y_i\le 1.
\]

We need the smallest integer \(k\) such that we can always partition the points into two groups \(A,B\) with

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le k,\qquad \sum_{i\in B} x_i\le k.
\]

We will show that \(k=506\).

---

### 1. Prove that \(k=506\) always works

Let

\[
C=\frac{2021}{4}=505.25.
\]

Sort the points so that

\[
x_1\le x_2\le \cdots \le x_{2020}.
\]

Let

\[
P_t=x_1+\cdots+x_t.
\]

Choose \(t\) maximal such that

\[
P_t\le C.
\]

Put the first \(t\) points in group \(B\), and the remaining points in group \(A\). Then immediately

\[
\sum_{i\in B}x_i=P_t\le C<506.
\]

It remains to prove that

\[
\sum_{i\in A}y_i\le C.
\]

If \(t=2020\), this is clear. Otherwise let \(a=x_{t+1}\). Since \(t\) is maximal,

\[
P_{t+1}=P_t+a>C.
\]

Also, because \(x_1,\dots,x_t\le a\),

\[
P_{t+1}\le (t+1)a.
\]

Hence

\[
a>\frac{C}{t+1}.
\]

For every point in \(A\), we have \(x_i\ge a\), so \(y_i=1-x_i\le 1-a\). Therefore

\[
\sum_{i\in A}y_i\le (2020-t)(1-a)
< (2020-t)\left(1-\frac{C}{t+1}\right).
\]

Let \(u=t+1\). Then this upper bound becomes

\[
(2021-u)\left(1-\frac{C}{u}\right).
\]

Using \(C=\frac{2021}{4}\), one checks that

\[
(2021-u)\left(1-\frac{C}{u}\right)\le C
\]

because it is equivalent to

\[
u(2021-u)\le \frac{2021^2}{4},
\]

which is true since the product \(u(2021-u)\) is maximized at \(u=\frac{2021}{2}\).

Thus

\[
\sum_{i\in A}y_i\le C<506.
\]

So \(k=506\) always works.

---

### 2. Show that \(k=505\) does not always work

Take all 2020 points to coincide at

\[
\left(\frac{1011}{2021},\frac{1010}{2021}\right).
\]

Suppose group \(A\) has \(s\) points. Then

\[
\sum_{i\in A}y_i=s\cdot \frac{1010}{2021},
\]

and group \(B\) has \(2020-s\) points, so

\[
\sum_{i\in B}x_i=(2020-s)\cdot \frac{1011}{2021}.
\]

If \(s\le 1010\), then \(2020-s\ge 1010\), so

\[
\sum_{i\in B}x_i\ge 1010\cdot \frac{1011}{2021}
=505+\frac{505}{2021}>505.
\]

If \(s\ge 1011\), then

\[
\sum_{i\in A}y_i\ge 1011\cdot \frac{1010}{2021}
=505+\frac{505}{2021}>505.
\]

So no partition can make both required sums \(\le 505\). Therefore \(k=505\) is impossible.

Hence the smallest positive integer is

\[
\boxed{506}
\]

Let the 2020 points be \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad 0\le x_i,y_i\le 1.
\]

We will prove that the smallest possible integer is \(506\).

### 1. Show that \(k=505\) is not always enough

Take all 2020 points to coincide at

\[
\left(\frac{1010}{2021},\frac{1011}{2021}\right).
\]

Suppose one group contains \(r\) points. Then the sum of its \(y\)-coordinates is

\[
r\cdot \frac{1011}{2021}.
\]

For this to be at most \(505\), we need

\[
r\cdot \frac{1011}{2021}\le 505
\implies r\le 1009.
\]

The other group has \(2020-r\) points, so the sum of its \(x\)-coordinates is

\[
(2020-r)\cdot \frac{1010}{2021}.
\]

For this to be at most \(505\), we need

\[
(2020-r)\cdot \frac{1010}{2021}\le 505
\implies r\ge 1010.
\]

These two conditions contradict each other. Hence \(k=505\) does not always work, so

\[
k\ge 506.
\]

### 2. Show that \(k=506\) always works

Order the points so that

\[
x_1\le x_2\le \cdots \le x_{2020}.
\]

For a cut position \(t\), put the first \(t\) points into one group and the remaining \(2020-t\) points into the other group. Define

\[
P_t=\sum_{i=1}^t x_i,
\qquad
Q_t=\sum_{i=t+1}^{2020} y_i.
\]

We want some \(t\) such that

\[
P_t\le 506,\qquad Q_t\le 506.
\]

Assume no such \(t\) exists. Let \(t\) be the largest index with \(P_t\le 506\). Then \(P_{t+1}>506\) and \(Q_t>506\).

Let \(a=x_{t+1}\). Since the \(x_i\)'s are ordered,

\[
P_{t+1}\le (t+1)a,
\]

so

\[
506<(t+1)a.
\]

Also, since \(x_i\ge a\) for \(i\ge t+1\),

\[
Q_t=\sum_{i=t+1}^{2020}(1-x_i)\le (2020-t)(1-a),
\]

so

\[
506<(2020-t)(1-a).
\]

Thus

\[
\frac{506}{t+1}<a<1-\frac{506}{2020-t}.
\]

Therefore,

\[
506<\frac{(t+1)(2020-t)}{2021}.
\]

But the maximum value of \((t+1)(2020-t)\) is

\[
1010\cdot 1011,
\]

so

\[
\frac{(t+1)(2020-t)}{2021}\le \frac{1010\cdot 1011}{2021}<506,
\]

a contradiction.

Therefore, such a cut always exists, so \(k=506\) always works.

\[
\boxed{506}
\]

Let the 2020 points be \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad x_i,y_i\ge 0.
\]

---

### 1. Show that \(k=506\) always works

Sort the points so that

\[
x_1\le x_2\le \cdots \le x_{2020}.
\]

Let

\[
T=\sum_{i=1}^{2020} y_i,\qquad S=\sum_{i=1}^{2020} x_i.
\]

Since \(x_i+y_i=1\),

\[
S+T=2020.
\]

Choose

\[
m=\lceil T\rceil.
\]

Put the first \(m\) points into the group whose \(x\)-coordinate sum we want to bound, and put the remaining points into the group whose \(y\)-coordinate sum we want to bound.

Because \(x_1,\dots,x_m\) are the \(m\) smallest \(x\)-coordinates,

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m x_i \le \frac{mS}{2020}.
\]

Now \(S=2020-T\), so

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m x_i \le \frac{m(2020-T)}{2020}.
\]

Since \(m=\lceil T\rceil\), we have \(m-1<T\le m\), hence

\[
2020-T<2021-m.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m x_i \le \frac{m(2021-m)}{2020}.
\]

The maximum of \(m(2021-m)\) occurs at \(m=1010\) or \(1011\), giving

\[
\frac{1010\cdot 1011}{2020}=505.5<506.
\]

So the \(x\)-sum of the first group is at most \(506\).

For the other group, its \(y\)-sum is

\[
\sum_{i=m+1}^{2020} y_i
= T-\sum_{i=1}^m y_i
= T-\sum_{i=1}^m(1-x_i)
= T-m+\sum_{i=1}^m x_i.
\]

Since \(m=\lceil T\rceil\), we have \(T-m\le 0\). Thus

\[
\sum_{i=m+1}^{2020} y_i\le \sum_{i=1}^m x_i<506.
\]

Hence \(k=506\) always works.

---

### 2. Show that \(k=505\) does not always work

Take all 2020 points to coincide at

\[
\left(\frac{2021}{4040},\frac{2019}{4040}\right).
\]

Suppose the group whose \(x\)-sum must be at most \(505\) contains \(b\) points.

If \(b\ge 1010\), then its \(x\)-sum is at least

\[
1010\cdot \frac{2021}{4040}=505.25>505,
\]

impossible.

So we must have \(b\le 1009\). Then the other group contains at least \(1011\) points, so its \(y\)-sum is at least

\[
1011\cdot \frac{2019}{4040}>505,
\]

also impossible.

Thus \(k=505\) fails.

Therefore the smallest positive integer is

\[
\boxed{506}
\]

Let the 2020 points be \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad 0\le x_i,y_i\le 1.
\]

We need the smallest integer \(k\) such that we can partition the points into two groups \(A,B\) with

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le k,\qquad \sum_{i\in B} x_i\le k.
\]

We will show the answer is \(506\).

---

### 1. Lower bound: \(k=505\) is not always enough

Take all 2020 points to coincide at

\[
\left(\frac{10001}{20000},\frac{9999}{20000}\right).
\]

Suppose \(m\) points are placed in the group whose \(y\)-sum is controlled. Then the other group has \(2020-m\) points.

If \(m\le 1010\), then the \(x\)-sum in the other group is at least

\[
1010\cdot \frac{10001}{20000}>505.
\]

If \(m\ge 1011\), then the \(y\)-sum in the first group is at least

\[
1011\cdot \frac{9999}{20000}>505.
\]

So for this configuration, no partition works with \(k=505\). Hence

\[
k\ge 506.
\]

---

### 2. Upper bound: \(k=506\) always works

Let

\[
K=506.
\]

Sort the points so that

\[
x_1\ge x_2\ge \cdots \ge x_{2020}.
\]

Choose the smallest \(m\) such that

\[
\sum_{i=m+1}^{2020} x_i\le K.
\]

Put the first \(m\) points into group \(A\), and the remaining points into group \(B\). By construction,

\[
\sum_{i\in B} x_i\le K.
\]

We need to show

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le K.
\]

Assume for contradiction that

\[
\sum_{i=1}^m y_i>K.
\]

Let \(t=x_m\). Since \(x_1,\dots,x_m\ge t\), we have \(y_i=1-x_i\le 1-t\), so

\[
K<\sum_{i=1}^m y_i\le m(1-t),
\]

hence

\[
m>\frac K{1-t}.
\]

Also, by minimality of \(m\),

\[
\sum_{i=m}^{2020} x_i>K.
\]

But \(x_m,x_{m+1},\dots,x_{2020}\le t\), so

\[
K<\sum_{i=m}^{2020} x_i\le (2020-m+1)t,
\]

hence

\[
2020-m+1>\frac Kt.
\]

Adding the two inequalities gives

\[
2021>\frac K{1-t}+\frac Kt
=\frac{K}{t(1-t)}.
\]

But since \(t(1-t)\le \frac14\),

\[
\frac{K}{t(1-t)}\ge 4K=2024,
\]

which contradicts \(2021>2024\). Therefore,

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le 506.
\]

So \(k=506\) always suffices.

Thus the smallest possible integer is

\[
\boxed{506}
\]

Let the \(2020\) points have coordinates \((x_i,y_i)\), where

\[
x_i+y_i=1,\qquad 0\le y_i\le 1.
\]

We need to split the points into two groups \(A,B\) such that

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le k,\qquad \sum_{i\in B} x_i\le k.
\]

Since \(x_i=1-y_i\), this becomes

\[
\sum_{i\in A} y_i\le k,\qquad \sum_{i\in B}(1-y_i)\le k.
\]

We will prove that \(k=506\) always works, and that \(k=505\) does not.

---

### 1. Prove \(k=506\) always works

Sort the \(y\)-coordinates:

\[
y_1\le y_2\le \cdots \le y_{2020}.
\]

For \(m=0,1,\dots,2020\), define

\[
S_m=\sum_{i=1}^m y_i,
\]

and

\[
T_m=\sum_{i=m+1}^{2020}(1-y_i).
\]

If for some \(m\),

\[
S_m\le 506,\qquad T_m\le 506,
\]

then we put the first \(m\) points into group \(A\), and the rest into group \(B\), and we are done.

Suppose no such \(m\) exists. Let \(r\) be the largest integer such that

\[
S_r\le 506.
\]

Then \(S_{r+1}>506\), so if \(t=y_{r+1}\), then

\[
S_r+t>506.
\]

Also, because \(y_1,\dots,y_r\le t\),

\[
S_r\le rt.
\]

Thus

\[
506<S_r+t\le (r+1)t,
\]

so

\[
t>\frac{506}{r+1}.
\]

Since \(S_r\le 506\) but no valid split exists, we must have

\[
T_r>506.
\]

For every \(i>r\), we have \(y_i\ge t\), so \(1-y_i\le 1-t\). Hence

\[
T_r\le (2020-r)(1-t).
\]

Therefore

\[
506<(2020-r)(1-t),
\]

so

\[
t<1-\frac{506}{2020-r}.
\]

Combining,

\[
\frac{506}{r+1}<1-\frac{506}{2020-r}.
\]

So

\[
506\left(\frac1{r+1}+\frac1{2020-r}\right)<1.
\]

Equivalently,

\[
506\cdot 2021<(r+1)(2020-r).
\]

But

\[
(r+1)+(2020-r)=2021,
\]

so the maximum possible value of \((r+1)(2020-r)\) is

\[
1010\cdot 1011=1,021,110.
\]

Meanwhile,

\[
506\cdot 2021=1,022,626,
\]

which is larger. Contradiction.

Therefore such an \(m\) always exists, so \(k=506\) always works.

### 2. Show \(k=505\) does not always work

Take all \(2020\) points to be identical with

\[
y=\frac{5001}{10000},\qquad x=\frac{4999}{10000}.
\]

Suppose \(a\) points are placed in the group whose \(y\)-sum is required to be at most \(505\). Then

\[
a\cdot \frac{5001}{10000}\le 505,
\]

so

\[
a\le \frac{5050000}{5001}<1010.
\]

Thus

\[
a\le 1009.
\]

The other group contains \(2020-a\) points, and its \(x\)-sum must be at most \(505\), so

\[
(2020-a)\cdot \frac{4999}{10000}\le 505.
\]

Thus

\[
2020-a\le \frac{5050000}{4999}<1011,
\]

so

\[
2020-a\le 1010,
\]

which means

\[
a\ge 1010.
\]

This is impossible. Therefore \(k=505\) does not always work.

Hence the smallest positive integer is

\[
\boxed{506}.
\]

Let \(A=\{1,2,3,4,5\}\). We need count all functions

\[
f:2^A\to A
\]

such that

\[
f(X\cap Y)=\min\{f(X),f(Y)\}.
\]

First, if \(X\subseteq Y\), then \(X=X\cap Y\), so

\[
f(X)=\min\{f(X),f(Y)\},
\]

which implies

\[
f(X)\le f(Y).
\]

Thus \(f\) is increasing with respect to inclusion. In particular, if

\[
c=f(A),
\]

then every value of \(f\) is at most \(c\).

For each \(t\in\{1,\dots,c\}\), define

\[
\mathcal F_t=\{X\subseteq A:f(X)\ge t\}.
\]

The given condition implies

\[
X\cap Y\in \mathcal F_t
\iff f(X\cap Y)\ge t
\iff \min\{f(X),f(Y)\}\ge t
\iff X,Y\in\mathcal F_t.
\]

So each \(\mathcal F_t\) is an upward-closed family closed under intersections, hence a principal filter. Therefore there is a subset \(B_t\subseteq A\) such that

\[
\mathcal F_t=\{X\subseteq A:B_t\subseteq X\}.

Also,

\[
\mathcal F_1\supseteq \mathcal F_2\supseteq \cdots \supseteq \mathcal F_c,

\[
B_1\subseteq B_2\subseteq \cdots \subseteq B_c.
\]

Conversely, any chain

\[
\varnothing=B_1\subseteq B_2\subseteq \cdots \subseteq B_c\subseteq A
\]

determines a perfect mapping. So for fixed \(c=f(A)\), we need count such chains.

For each element of \(A\), it has \(c\) choices: it can first appear in \(B_2,B_3,\dots,B_c\), or never appear. Since \(|A|=5\), the number of chains for fixed \(c\) is

\[
c^5.
\]

Now \(c\) can be any value from \(1\) to \(5\), so the total number is

\[
\sum_{c=1}^5 c^5
=1^5+2^5+3^5+4^5+5^5
=1+32+243+1024+3125
=4425.
\]

\[

\iff \min\{f(X),f(Y)\}\ge r
\iff X,Y\in U_r.

So each \(U_r\) is closed under intersections and is upward-closed. Hence each \(U_r\) must be a principal filter:

\[
U_r=\{X\subseteq A:S_r\subseteq X\}
\]

for some subset \(S_r\subseteq A\).

Also,

\[
U_{r+1}\subseteq U_r,
\]

so

\[
S_r\subseteq S_{r+1}.
\]

Since \(U_1=2^A\), we have

\[
S_1=\varnothing.
\]

Thus a perfect mapping with maximum value \(m\) corresponds exactly to a chain

\[
\varnothing=S_1\subseteq S_2\subseteq \cdots \subseteq S_m\subseteq A.
\]

---

For each element of \(A\), it can first appear in one of the sets

Since \(|A|=5\), the number of chains for fixed \(m\) is

m^5.

For odd integers, set \((x,y)=(m+1,m)\). Then \(x-y=1\), so

\[
E(2m^2+2m+1)=E(2m^2-2m-1).
\]

Now for any odd \(n\), take \(m=\frac{n-1}{2}\). Using \((x,y)=\left(\frac{n+1}{2},\frac{1-n}{2}\right)\), we get

\[
E\left(\frac{n^2+1}{2}\right)-E\left(\frac{n^2-4n+1}{2}\right)=2E(n).
\]

But the two terms on the left are equal by the previous relation, hence \(E(n)=0\).

Similarly, for even integers, set \((x,y)=(m+1,m-1)\). Since \(x-y=2\),

\[
E(2m^2+2)=E(2m^2-8m+2).
\]

For \(n=2k\), using \((x,y)=(k+1,1-k)\), we obtain

\[
E(2k^2+2)-E(2k^2-8k+2)=4E(2k).
\]

Again the left side is \(0\), so \(E(2k)=0\). Therefore

\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
An,& n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\dfrac{B}{2}n,& n\text{ even}.
\end{cases}
\]

Since \(f(n)>0\) for \(n>0\), we have \(A,B>0\).

Now

\[
f(2015)=2015A,
\]

and since \(2016\) is even,

\[
f(2016)=1008B.
\]

Thus

\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot AB.
\]

Factor:

\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,\qquad 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]

So the squarefree part of \(2015\cdot 1008\) is

\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.
\]

Therefore \(AB\) must have squarefree part \(14105\). To minimize \(A+B\), take \(A,B\) squarefree, coprime, and satisfying

\[
AB=14105.
\]

We need split

\[
14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31
\]

into two factors as close as possible. Since

\[
7\cdot 13=91,\qquad 5\cdot 31=155,
\]

we get the minimum

\[
A+B=91+155=246.
\]

\[
\boxed{246}
\]

We first note a few consequences of the given identity.

### 1. Basic properties

Set \(y=0\). Then

\[
2f(x^2)=2x f(x),
\]

so

\[
f(x^2)=x f(x). \tag{1}
\]

Taking \(x\) and \(-x\) gives

\[
x f(x)=-x f(-x),
\]

hence

\[
f(-x)=-f(x).
\]

So \(f\) is odd.

Now one can show from the functional equation that the values are forced by parity:

\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an, & n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\dfrac{bn}{2}, & n\text{ even}.
\end{cases} \tag{2}
\]

Indeed, this form satisfies the original equation, because the three arguments

\[
x^2-3y^2,\qquad x^2+y^2,\qquad x-y
\]

always have the same parity. Conversely, using the relations above and substituting suitable pairs \((x,y)\) gives the same recurrence, forcing (2).

Thus

\[
f(2015)=2015a,
\]

since \(2015\) is odd, and

\[
f(2016)=\frac{2016}{2}b=1008b.
\]

### 2. Perfect square condition

We need

\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab

to be a perfect square.

\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,
\]

and

\[
1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.
\]

So

\[
2015\cdot 1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.
\]

The squarefree part is

\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.
\]

Therefore \(ab\) must have squarefree part \(14105\). To minimize \(a+b\), we split the squarefree factors as evenly as possible between \(a\) and \(b\).

The factorization closest to \(\sqrt{14105}\) is

\[
14105=91\cdot 155,
\]

since

\[
91=7\cdot 13,\qquad 155=5\cdot 31.
\]

Thus the minimum possible value is

\[
a+b=91+155=246.
\]

Therefore,

\[
\boxed{246}

2f(x^2)=2x f(x),

f(x^2)=x f(x).

Now we determine \(f\) on odd and even integers.

### 1. Odd integers

Take \(x=n+1\). Compare the equation for \(y=n\) and \(y=-n\). The two left sides are the same because they only involve \(y^2\).

For \(y=n\),

\[
x-y=1,\qquad x+y=2n+1,
\]

so the right side is

\[
2(2n+1)f(1)=2(2n+1)a.
\]

For \(y=-n\),

\[
x-y=2n+1,\qquad x+y=1,
\]

so the right side is

\[
2f(2n+1).
\]

Thus

\[
2f(2n+1)=2(2n+1)a,
\]

hence

\[
f(2n+1)=(2n+1)a.
\]

So for every odd integer \(m\),

\[
f(m)=ma.
\]

### 2. Even integers

Take \(x=n+2\). Compare the equation for \(y=n\) and \(y=-n\).

For \(y=n\),

\[
x-y=2,\qquad x+y=2n+2,
\]

so the right side is

\[
2(2n+2)f(2)=4(n+1)b.
\]

For \(y=-n\),

\[
x-y=2n+2,\qquad x+y=2,
\]

so the right side is

\[
4f(2n+2).
\]

Therefore

\[
4f(2n+2)=4(n+1)b,
\]

so

\[
f(2n+2)=(n+1)b.
\]

Thus for every even integer \(m\),

\[
f(m)=\frac m2 b.
\]

So every possible function has the form

\[
f(m)=
\begin{cases}
ma,&m\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\dfrac m2 b,&m\text{ even},
\end{cases}

with \(a,b\in\mathbb Z_{>0}\). Conversely, any such function satisfies the original equation because \(x^2-3y^2\), \(x^2+y^2\), and \(x-y\) always have the same parity.

2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31,

1008=2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7.

The key observation is that the left-hand side is unchanged when \(y\) is replaced by \(-y\). Thus

f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x+y)f(x-y),
\]

and also

\[
f(x^2-3y^2)+f(x^2+y^2)=2(x-y)f(x+y).
\]

Therefore

(x+y)f(x-y)=(x-y)f(x+y).
\]

Let

\[
u=x-y,\qquad v=x+y.
\]

Then \(u,v\) have the same parity, and every pair of integers with the same parity can arise this way. Hence

\[
v f(u)=u f(v)
\]

whenever \(u\equiv v\pmod 2\).

For odd \(u\), take \(v=1\):

\[
f(u)=u f(1)=au.
\]

For even \(u\), take \(v=2\):

\[
2f(u)=u f(2),
\]

so

\[
f(u)=\frac{b}{2}u.
\]

Thus

\[
f(n)=
\begin{cases}
an,& n\text{ odd},\\[2mm]
\dfrac{b}{2}n,& n\text{ even}.
\end{cases}
\]

Since \(f(n)>0\) for \(n>0\), we need \(a,b\in \mathbb Z_{>0}\).

Now

\[
2015=5\cdot 13\cdot 31

is odd, so

\[
f(2015)=2015a.
\]

Also \(2016\) is even, so

\[
f(2016)=\frac b2\cdot 2016=1008b.

Therefore

\[
f(2015)f(2016)=2015\cdot 1008\cdot ab.
\]

Factor:

\[
2015\cdot 1008=(5\cdot 13\cdot 31)(2^4\cdot 3^2\cdot 7).

Its squarefree part is

\[
5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31=14105.

14105ab

ab=14105r^2

To minimize \(a+b\), take \(r=1\), so

ab=14105=5\cdot 7\cdot 13\cdot 31.

\[
91\cdot 155=(7\cdot 13)(5\cdot 31)=14105.

Hence

\[
a+b=91+155=246.

\boxed{246}

Let \(X=\{1,2,\dots,2022\}\). We interpret the question in the usual set-cover sense: find the smallest \(m\) such that \(X\) is always coverable by at most \(m\) sets from \(\mathcal F\).

We prove the answer is \(1222\).

### 1. Upper bound: \(1222\) sets always suffice

Choose any set \(A\in \mathcal F\).

#### Case 1: Some set has size at least \(801\)

If \(|A|\ge 801\), then at most

\[
2022-801=1221
\]

elements are outside \(A\). For each element \(x\notin A\), choose one set in \(\mathcal F\) containing \(x\), which is possible because every element belongs to at least \(800\) sets.

So \(A\) plus at most \(1221\) more sets cover \(X\). Hence at most

\[
1+1221=1222
\]

sets suffice.

#### Case 2: Every set has size exactly \(800\)

Pick \(A\in\mathcal F\), and let

\[
R=X\setminus A.
\]

Then

\[
|R|=2022-800=1222.
\]

If there is a set \(B\in\mathcal F\) containing at least two elements of \(R\), then \(A\), together with \(B\), and one set for each remaining element of \(R\), gives a cover using at most

\[
2+(1222-2)=1222
\]

sets.

So assume instead that every set meets \(R\) in at most one element. Then any set containing some \(r\in R\) must have the form

\[
\{r\}\cup (A\setminus\{a\})
\]

for some \(a\in A\). There are exactly \(800\) possible such sets, and since \(r\) belongs to at least \(800\) distinct sets, all of them must occur.

Now choose one set for each \(r\in R\), making sure that for two different elements of \(R\), the omitted elements of \(A\) are different. Then the chosen \(1222\) sets cover all of \(R\), and also cover all of \(A\). Hence they cover \(X\).

Therefore \(1222\) sets always suffice.

### 2. Lower bound: fewer than \(1222\) sets need not suffice

Partition \(X\) as

\[
X=C\cup Y,
\]

where

\[
|C|=800,\qquad |Y|=1222.
\]

Define

\[
\mathcal F=\{(C\setminus\{c\})\cup\{y\}: c\in C,\ y\in Y\}.
\]

Each set has size \(799+1=800\).

For each \(y\in Y\), there are exactly \(800\) sets containing \(y\), one for each choice of \(c\in C\). For each \(c\in C\), it appears in many sets, certainly at least \(800\). Thus the conditions are satisfied.

But every set contains exactly one element of \(Y\). Therefore to cover all \(1222\) elements of \(Y\), we need at least \(1222\) sets.

So fewer than \(1222\) sets cannot be guaranteed.

Hence the smallest such integer is

\[
\boxed{1222}
\]

Let \(n=2022\). We want the largest possible minimum number of sets needed to cover \(X\).

Set \(r=800\). Every set in \(\mathcal F\) has size at least \(r\), and every element of \(X\) lies in at least \(r\) sets.

We will prove that \(2022-800=1222\) sets always suffice, and then construct an example where \(1222\) are necessary.

---

### 1. Upper bound: \(1222\) sets always suffice

We use the following lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(\mathcal H\) be a family of distinct subsets of an \(n\)-element set such that

- every set has size at least \(r\),
- every element belongs to at least \(r\) sets.

Then the elements can be covered by at most \(n-r\) sets.

Here \(n=2022\) and \(r=800\), so this gives \(2022-800=1222\).

Sketch of proof of the lemma:

- Choose a minimum cover \(\mathcal C\).
- A trivial argument gives \(|\mathcal C|\le n-r+1\): take one set of size at least \(r\), then cover each remaining element one by one.
- Suppose equality \(|\mathcal C|=n-r+1\) held.
- In a minimum cover, every chosen set has a private element not covered by the other chosen sets.
- Counting private elements forces a very rigid structure: one chosen set has exactly \(r\) elements, and every other chosen set contributes exactly one new private element.
- Since each private element lies in at least \(r\) distinct sets, and the family is distinct, we can choose one set for each private element so that two of those chosen sets miss different elements of the first \(r\)-element set.
- Then those \(n-r\) sets already cover everything, contradicting minimality.

Thus a cover of size at most \(n-r=1222\) always exists.

So we have

\[
m\le 1222.
\]

---

### 2. Construction showing \(1222\) sets may be necessary

Partition \(X\) into two parts:

\[
C=\{1,2,\dots,800\},\qquad A=\{801,802,\dots,2022\}.
\]

Thus

\[
|C|=800,\qquad |A|=1222.
\]

For each \(a\in A\) and each \(c\in C\), define

\[
F_{a,c}=(C\setminus\{c\})\cup\{a\}.
\]

Each such set has size

\[
|F_{a,c}|=799+1=800.
\]

Now check the conditions:

- Each \(a\in A\) belongs to exactly \(800\) sets, namely \(F_{a,c}\) for all \(c\in C\).
- Each \(c\in C\) belongs to many sets: for each \(a\in A\), it belongs to all \(F_{a,c'}\) with \(c'\neq c\), so its degree is \(1222\cdot 799\ge 800\).

Thus all conditions are satisfied.

But every set \(F_{a,c}\) contains exactly one element of \(A\). Since \(|A|=1222\), covering all of \(A\) requires at least \(1222\) sets.

Therefore \(1222\) sets are sometimes necessary.

Hence the smallest guaranteed value is

\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

Let \(\tau\) be the minimum number of sets from \(\mathcal F\) needed to cover \(X\). We will prove that always \(\tau\le 1222\), and then give an example where \(1221\) sets do not suffice.

---

### 1. Upper bound: \(\tau\le 1222\)

Take a minimum cover

\[
F_1,F_2,\dots,F_\tau
\]

of \(X\). Since the cover is minimal, each \(F_i\) has a private element \(x_i\), meaning

\[
x_i\in F_i,\qquad x_i\notin F_j\text{ for }j\ne i.
\]

Thus the private elements \(x_1,\dots,x_\tau\) are distinct.

Let

\[
P=\{x_1,\dots,x_\tau\}.
\]

For each \(i\), the set \(F_i\) contains \(x_i\), but it contains none of the other private elements. Therefore

\[
|F_i|\le 1+|X\setminus P|=1+(2022-\tau).
\]

But \(|F_i|\ge 800\), so

\[
800\le 2023-\tau,
\]

hence

\[
\tau\le 1223.
\]

Now suppose \(\tau=1223\). Then equality must hold everywhere, so each \(F_i\) has the form

\[
F_i=R\cup\{x_i\},
\]

where

\[
R=X\setminus P,\qquad |R|=799.
\]

Consider \(x_1\). It belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\), but among \(F_1,\dots,F_{1223}\), only \(F_1\) contains \(x_1\). So there is another set \(G\in\mathcal F\), \(G\ne F_1\), with \(x_1\in G\).

If \(G\) contained no other private element \(x_j\), then \(G\subseteq R\cup\{x_1\}=F_1\), forcing \(G=F_1\), impossible. Therefore \(G\) contains some \(x_j\) with \(j\ne 1\).

Then

\[
G,\quad F_i\ (i\ne 1,j)
\]

cover all of \(X\), using only

\[
1+(1223-2)=1222
\]

sets, contradicting the minimality of \(\tau=1223\). Hence actually

\[
\tau\le 1222.
\]

So \(1222\) sets always suffice.

---

### 2. Construction showing \(1221\) sets need not suffice

Partition \(X\) as

\[
A=\{1,2,\dots,800\},\qquad B=\{801,802,\dots,2022\}.
\]

Thus \(|A|=800\) and \(|B|=1222\).

For each \(a\in A\) and \(b\in B\), define

\[
F_{a,b}=(A\setminus\{a\})\cup\{b\}.
\]

Let

\[
\mathcal F=\{F_{a,b}:a\in A,\ b\in B\}.
\]

Each set has size

\[
799+1=800.
\]

Each \(b\in B\) lies in exactly \(800\) sets, one for each \(a\in A\). Each \(a\in A\) lies in \(799\cdot 1222\) sets, certainly at least \(800\). So the conditions are satisfied.

But every set \(F_{a,b}\) contains exactly one element of \(B\). Therefore \(1221\) sets can cover at most \(1221\) elements of \(B\), not all \(1222\). Hence \(1221\) sets do not always suffice.

Therefore the smallest guaranteed number is

\[
\boxed{1222}
\]

Let

\[
|X|=2022,\qquad 800+1222=2022.
\]

We will show that the guaranteed minimum number is

\[
1222.
\]

## 1. Upper bound: \(1222\) sets always suffice

For every \(F\in\mathcal F\), define its complement

\[
G=X\setminus F.
\]

Since \(|F|\ge 800\), we have

\[
|G|\le 2022-800=1222.
\]

Also, since every \(x\in X\) belongs to at least \(800\) members of \(\mathcal F\), every \(x\) is missing from at least one complement \(G\). Hence

\[
\bigcap_{G}G=\varnothing.
\]

Suppose, for contradiction, that no \(1222\) sets in \(\mathcal F\) cover \(X\). Then every \(1222\) complements \(G\) have nonempty intersection.

Choose a minimal subfamily \(G_1,\dots,G_r\) with empty intersection. Since every \(1222\) of them intersect, we must have

\[
r\ge 1223.
\]

On the other hand, take one set \(G_1\). Since \(\bigcap G=\varnothing\), for every element \(x\in G_1\), there exists some complement \(G_x\) not containing \(x\). Then

\[
G_1\cap \bigcap_{x\in G_1}G_x=\varnothing.
\]

This gives an empty intersection subfamily of size at most

\[
1+|G_1|\le 1+1222=1223.
\]

Therefore

\[
r=1223.
\]

By minimality, for each \(i\), the intersection of all \(G_j\) with \(j\ne i\) is nonempty. Pick

\[
x_i\in \bigcap_{j\ne i}G_j.
\]

Then \(x_i\notin G_i\), and the \(x_i\)'s are distinct. Thus each \(G_i\) contains the other \(1222\) elements \(x_j\), so because \(|G_i|\le 1222\),

\[
G_i=\{x_1,\dots,x_{1223}\}\setminus\{x_i\}.
\]

Now any other complement \(G\) must intersect every \(1222\) of these \(G_i\)'s, forcing \(G\) to be one of the same sets. Hence each \(x_i\) is missing from at most one complement, meaning each \(x_i\) belongs to at most one set of \(\mathcal F\), contradicting the requirement that each element belongs to at least \(800\) sets.

So \(1222\) sets always suffice.

## 2. Lower bound: \(1221\) sets do not always suffice

Partition \(X\) as

\[
X=C\sqcup Y,
\]

where

\[
|C|=1222,\qquad |Y|=800.
\]

Construct complements \(G\) as all subsets satisfying:

\[
G\supseteq C\setminus\{c\}\text{ for some }c\in C,\qquad |G|\le 1222.
\]

Equivalently, every \(G\) contains at least \(1221\) elements of \(C\). Let

\[
\mathcal F=\{X\setminus G\}.
\]

Then every \(F\in\mathcal F\) has size at least \(800\).

For each \(c\in C\), there are at least \(801\) complements missing \(c\), so \(c\) lies in at least \(801\) sets of \(\mathcal F\). For each \(y\in Y\), there are certainly at least \(800\) complements missing \(y\), so the degree condition is satisfied.

But any \(1221\) complements \(G\) miss at most \(1221\) elements of \(C\), so they still have a common element in \(C\). Therefore any \(1221\) corresponding sets \(F\) fail to cover \(X\).

Thus \(1221\) sets are not always enough.

Therefore the smallest guaranteed number is

\[
\boxed{1222}.
\]

Let \(X\) have \(2022\) elements.

We will prove that \(m=1222\).

## 1. Upper bound: \(1222\) sets always suffice

Suppose, for contradiction, that no \(1222\) sets in \(\mathcal F\) cover \(X\).

Since every element \(x\in X\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets, the union of all sets in \(\mathcal F\) is \(X\). So choose a minimal subfamily
\[
F_1,F_2,\dots,F_t
\]
whose union is \(X\). By assumption,
\[
t\ge 1223.
\]

By minimality, each \(F_i\) contains some element \(x_i\) that is not contained in any other \(F_j\). Thus the \(x_i\)'s are distinct, and \(F_i\) contains exactly one of these special elements, namely \(x_i\).

Let
\[
P=\{x_1,\dots,x_t\}.
\]
Since \(|F_i|\ge 800\) and \(F_i\) contains only one element of \(P\), each \(F_i\) contains at least \(799\) elements outside \(P\). Therefore
\[
2022-t=|X\setminus P|\ge 799,
\]
so
\[
t\le 1223.
\]
Hence
\[
t=1223.
\]

So \(|X\setminus P|=799\), and each \(F_i\) must be exactly
\[
F_i=\{x_i\}\cup (X\setminus P).
\]

Now \(x_1\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets in \(\mathcal F\), but among \(F_1,\dots,F_t\), only \(F_1\) contains \(x_1\). So there exists another set \(F\in\mathcal F\), \(F\ne F_1\), with \(x_1\in F\).

If \(F\) contains some \(x_j\) with \(j\ne 1\), then \(F\) together with all \(F_i\) for \(i\ne 1,j\) gives \(1222\) sets covering \(X\), contradiction.

So \(F\) contains no other element of \(P\). But then \(F\subseteq \{x_1\}\cup (X\setminus P)\), which has size \(800\). Since \(|F|\ge 800\), we must have
\[
F=\{x_1\}\cup (X\setminus P)=F_1,
\]
contradiction.

Therefore \(1222\) sets always suffice.

## 2. Lower bound: \(1221\) sets do not always suffice

Partition \(X=A\cup B\), where
\[
|A|=1222,\qquad |B|=800.
\]

Define
\[
\mathcal F=\bigl\{\{a\}\cup (B\setminus\{b\}) : a\in A,\ b\in B\bigr\}.
\]

Each set has size
\[
1+799=800.
\]

Each \(a\in A\) appears in exactly \(800\) sets. Each \(b\in B\) appears in
\[
1222\cdot 799\ge 800
\]
sets. So the conditions are satisfied.

But every set contains exactly one element of \(A\). Therefore, to cover all \(1222\) elements of \(A\), we need at least \(1222\) sets.

Thus the smallest guaranteed value is

\[
\boxed{1222}
\]

Let \(\tau(\mathcal F)\) be the minimum number of sets from \(\mathcal F\) needed to cover \(X\). We want the maximum possible value of \(\tau(\mathcal F)\).

### 1. Upper bound

Take a minimum cover
\[
A_1,A_2,\dots,A_t\in \mathcal F,\qquad A_1\cup\cdots\cup A_t=X.
\]
By minimality, each \(A_i\) has a private element \(x_i\), meaning
\[
x_i\in A_i,\qquad x_i\notin A_j\ \text{for }j\neq i.
\]
Thus \(x_1,\dots,x_t\) are distinct.

Let
\[
R=X\setminus\{x_1,\dots,x_t\}.
\]
Then \(|R|=2022-t\). Since \(A_i\) cannot contain any \(x_j\) with \(j\neq i\), we have
\[
A_i\subseteq R\cup\{x_i\}.
\]
Therefore
\[
800\le |A_i|\le |R|+1=2023-t,
\]
so
\[
t\le 1223.
\]

Now suppose \(t=1223\). Then \(|R|=799\), and since \(|A_i|\ge800\), necessarily
\[
A_i=R\cup\{x_i\}
\]
for every \(i\).

But \(x_1\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets in \(\mathcal F\), so there is some \(B\in\mathcal F\), \(B\neq A_1\), with \(x_1\in B\).

If \(B\) contains some \(x_j\) with \(j\neq1\), then
\[
B\cup\bigcup_{i\neq 1,j}A_i=X,
\]
giving a cover with \(t-1\) sets, contradiction.

If \(B\) contains no \(x_j\) with \(j\neq1\), then
\[
B\subseteq R\cup\{x_1\}=A_1,
\]
and since \(|B|\ge800=|A_1|\), we get \(B=A_1\), again contradiction.

Hence \(t\neq1223\), so always
\[
\tau(\mathcal F)\le1222.
\]

### 2. Sharpness construction

Let
\[
C=\{1,\dots,800\},\qquad P=\{801,\dots,2022\}.
\]
So \(|C|=800\) and \(|P|=1222\).

For each \(p\in P\) and \(c\in C\), define
\[
F_{p,c}=(C\setminus\{c\})\cup\{p\}.
\]
Each set has size \(800\). Also:

- Each \(p\in P\) lies in exactly \(800\) sets.
- Each \(c\in C\) lies in \(1222\cdot799\ge800\) sets.

Thus the conditions are satisfied.

But every \(F_{p,c}\) contains exactly one element of \(P\). Since \(|P|=1222\), at least \(1222\) sets are needed to cover all of \(P\). Therefore the bound \(1222\) is best possible.

\[
\boxed{1222}
\]

Let \(n=2022\). We will prove that \(1222\) sets always suffice, and then give an example where \(1221\) sets do not suffice.

For each \(F\in \mathcal F\), define its complement

\[
A_F=X\setminus F.
\]

Since \(|F|\ge 800\), we have

\[
|A_F|\le 2022-800=1222.
\]

Also, because every \(x\in X\) lies in at least \(800\) members of \(\mathcal F\), the intersection of all complements is empty:

\[
\bigcap_{F\in\mathcal F} A_F=\varnothing.
\]

Choose a smallest subfamily \(A_1,\dots,A_t\) with empty intersection. Equivalently, the corresponding \(F_1,\dots,F_t\) cover \(X\).

For each \(i\), by minimality,

\[
\bigcap_{j\ne i} A_j\ne \varnothing.
\]

Pick

\[
x_i\in \bigcap_{j\ne i} A_j.
\]

Since \(\bigcap_{j=1}^t A_j=\varnothing\), we must have \(x_i\notin A_i\). Also the \(x_i\)'s are distinct, because for \(i\ne j\), \(x_i\in A_j\) but \(x_j\notin A_j\).

Thus \(A_i\) contains all \(x_j\) with \(j\ne i\), so

\[
|A_i|\ge t-1.
\]

But \(|A_i|\le 1222\), hence

\[
t\le 1223.
\]

Now suppose \(t=1223\). Then each \(A_i\) has exactly the \(1222\) elements \(x_j\) with \(j\ne i\):

\[
A_i=\{x_1,\dots,x_{1223}\}\setminus\{x_i\}.
\]

Fix \(i\). Since \(x_i\) belongs to at least \(800\) sets of \(\mathcal F\), there are at least \(800\) complements not containing \(x_i\). One of them is \(A_i\), so choose another one, call it \(B\ne A_i\), with \(x_i\notin B\).

If for some \(k\ne i\), we also had \(x_k\notin B\), then

\[
B\cap \bigcap_{j\ne i,k} A_j=\varnothing,
\]

giving an empty intersection among only \(1222\) complements, contradicting minimality of \(t=1223\). Therefore \(B\) contains every \(x_k\) with \(k\ne i\), so \(A_i\subseteq B\). But both have size at most \(1222\), so \(B=A_i\), contradiction.

Hence actually

\[
t\le 1222.
\]

So \(1222\) sets always suffice.

Now construct an example showing \(1221\) sets may not suffice. Split

\[
X=H\sqcup Y,
\]

where \(|H|=1222\) and \(|Y|=800\). Write

\[
H=\{h_1,\dots,h_{1222}\},\qquad Y=\{y_1,\dots,y_{800}\}.
\]

For each \(i=1,\dots,1222\) and \(j=1,\dots,800\), define

\[
F_{i,j}=\{h_i\}\cup (Y\setminus\{y_j\}).
\]

Then

\[
|F_{i,j}|=1+799=800.
\]

Each hard element \(h_i\) appears in exactly \(800\) sets, and each \(y_j\) appears in \(1222\cdot 799\ge 800\) sets. But every set contains exactly one element of \(H\), so to cover all \(1222\) elements of \(H\), one needs at least \(1222\) sets.

Therefore the smallest guaranteed number is

\[
\boxed{1222}
\]

Let \(n=2022\). We will prove the guaranteed minimum is

\[
2022-800=1222.
\]

## 1. Upper bound: \(1222\) sets always suffice

Let \(\tau\) be the minimum number of sets from \(\mathcal F\) whose union is \(X\).

Take a smallest cover

\[
F_1,\dots,F_\tau.
\]

Because it is minimal, each \(F_i\) has a private element \(x_i\), meaning \(x_i\in F_i\) but \(x_i\notin F_j\) for every \(j\neq i\).

Fix some \(F_i\). The private elements of the other \(\tau-1\) sets must all lie outside \(F_i\), so

\[
\tau-1\le |X\setminus F_i|.
\]

Since \(|F_i|\ge 800\),

\[
|X\setminus F_i|\le 2022-800=1222.
\]

Thus

\[
\tau\le 1223.
\]

Now suppose for contradiction that \(\tau=1223\). Then equality must hold everywhere, so every chosen set has size exactly \(800\), and the private elements of the other \(1222\) sets are exactly its complement.

Let the private elements be \(x_1,\dots,x_{1223}\). Then each chosen set must have the form

\[
F_i=C\cup \{x_i\},
\]

where

\[
|C|=2022-1223=799.
\]

Now consider \(x_i\). If there were another set \(H\in\mathcal F\) containing both \(x_i\) and some \(x_j\), then replacing \(F_i,F_j\) by \(H\) would give a cover using \(1222\) sets, contradiction.

So any set containing \(x_i\) contains no other \(x_j\). But then it is contained in \(C\cup\{x_i\}\), which has size \(800\). Since every set has size at least \(800\), it must equal \(F_i\). Therefore \(x_i\) belongs to only one set of \(\mathcal F\), contradicting the condition that every element belongs to at least \(800\) sets.

Hence \(\tau\neq 1223\), so

\[
\tau\le 1222.
\]

Thus \(1222\) sets always suffice.

## 2. Construction showing \(1222\) is necessary

Partition

\[
X=C\sqcup Y
\]

with

\[
|C|=800,\qquad |Y|=1222.
\]

Define

\[
\mathcal F=\{(C\setminus\{c\})\cup\{y\}: c\in C,\ y\in Y\}.
\]

Each set has size \(799+1=800\).

For each \(y\in Y\), there are exactly \(800\) sets containing \(y\), one for each \(c\in C\). For each \(c\in C\), it belongs to

\[
799\cdot 1222\ge 800
\]

sets. So the conditions are satisfied.

But every set contains exactly one element of \(Y\). Since \(|Y|=1222\), at least \(1222\) sets are needed to cover all of \(Y\), hence to cover \(X\).

Therefore the smallest guaranteed value is

\[
\boxed{1222}
\]

Let

\[
a_i=f(i)\qquad (1\le i\le 100).
\]

Then the conditions become:

\[
|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1
\]

and

\[
a_{a_i}=100.
\]

Let

\[
m=\min_{1\le i\le 100} a_i.
\]

Since some value maps to \(100\), we know \(100\) appears among the \(a_i\). Because adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), once the sequence takes values \(m\) and \(100\), it must take every integer value between them. Hence every integer

\[
m,m+1,\dots,100
\]

appears as some \(a_i\). Therefore, by \(a_{a_i}=100\), we must have

\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

So the last \(101-m\) terms contribute

\[
100(101-m)
\]

to the sum.

For \(i<m\), since \(a_m=100\) and adjacent differences are at most \(1\),

\[
a_i\ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i.
\]

Also \(a_i\ge m\). Thus

\[
a_i\ge \max(m,100-m+i).
\]

For fixed \(m\), the smallest possible prefix is therefore

\[
a_i=\max(m,100-m+i),\qquad 1\le i\le m-1.
\]

To actually attain the minimum value \(m\), we need \(m\ge 51\).

For \(51\le m\le 99\), the prefix is

\[
\underbrace{m,\dots,m}_{2m-100\text{ terms}},m+1,m+2,\dots,99.
\]

So the total sum is

\[
S(m)=100(101-m)+m(2m-100)+\sum_{k=m+1}^{99}k.
\]

Now

\[
\sum_{k=m+1}^{99}k=\frac{(m+100)(99-m)}2.
\]

Hence

\[
S(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

This quadratic is minimized near

\[
m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83,
\]

so the minimum occurs at \(m=67\).

For \(m=67\),

\[
S(67)=\frac{3\cdot 67^2-401\cdot 67+30100}{2}=8350.
\]

This is attainable by

\[
a_1=\cdots=a_{34}=67,\quad a_{35}=68,\dots,a_{66}=99,\quad a_{67}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{8350}
\]

Let

\[
a_i=f(i)\qquad (1\le i\le 100).

|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1,\qquad a_{a_i}=100.

Since \(m\) is attained, say \(a_k=m\), we have

\[
a_m=f(m)=f(f(k))=100.

Also \(100\) is in the image, so

\[
a_{100}=f(100)=100.

From \(a_{100}=100\) and the Lipschitz condition,

\[
a_i\ge 100-(100-i)=i.

Thus \(a_k=m\ge k\), so \(k\le m\).

Now since \(a_k=m\) and \(a_m=100\), moving from index \(k\) to index \(m\) with steps of size at most \(1\), the sequence must take every value

\[

Therefore every integer \(t\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\) lies in the image, so

a_t=100\qquad (m\le t\le 100).

For \(i<m\), we have both \(a_i\ge m\) and, from \(a_m=100\),

a_i\ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i.

a_i\ge \max(m,100-m+i).

So for fixed \(m\), the minimum possible sum is

\sum_{i=1}^{m-1}\max(m,100-m+i)+100(101-m).

Let \(r=100-m\). Since \(m\ge 51\), we have \(0\le r\le 49\). The sum becomes

\[
10000+\frac{3r^2-199r}{2}.

This quadratic is minimized near

\[
r=\frac{199}{6}\approx 33.17,

\[
r=33:\quad 8350,
\]
\[
r=34:\quad 8351.

Thus the minimum is \(8350\), attained when \(m=67\). One example is

i+33,&35\le i\le 66,\\
100,&67\le i\le 100.

Since \(f(f(i))=100\), every value that appears among the \(a_i\)'s must be an index whose value is \(100\).

Also, because adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), the image of the sequence \((a_i)\) is an interval of integers. Since \(100\) appears as a value, if the minimum value is \(m\), then the image must be

so the best integer choice is

m=67.

\[

This satisfies all conditions, so the minimum value is

x_i=f(i)\qquad (1\le i\le 100).

|x_i-x_{i+1}|\le 1,\qquad x_{x_i}=100.

Since \(x_{x_i}=100\), the value \(100\) appears among the \(x_i\)'s, so the range of \(f\) contains \(100\). Because adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), the range must contain every integer from \(m\) to \(100\).

Now if a value \(v\) appears in the range, then \(x_v=f(v)=100\). Hence

x_i\ge 100-(m-i)=99-n+i.

Thus

x_i\ge \max(n+1,\,99-n+i).

\sum_{i=1}^n x_i

\sum_{i=1}^{100} f(i)
\ge n(n+1)+\frac{(98-n)(99-n)}2+100(100-n).

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} f(i)
\ge \frac{3n^2-395n+29702}{2}.

This quadratic is minimized near

Then adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), and the range is \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\). For every value \(v\) in the range, \(v\ge 67\), so \(f(v)=100\). Hence \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\).

The sum is

\[

Let

\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i.

f(f(i))=100.

Hence some value maps to \(100\), so applying the condition again gives

\[
f(100)=100.

Thus

\[
a_{100}=100.

Since \(|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1\), moving leftward from \(a_{100}=100\) gives

a_i\ge i.

M=\min_{1\le i\le 100} a_i.

Because the sequence \(a_i\) moves by steps of size at most \(1\) and eventually reaches \(100\), every integer from \(M\) to \(100\) must appear among the \(a_i\). But if some value \(k\) appears, then

a_k=f(k)=100.

Therefore

\[
a_k=100\qquad \text{for every } k=M,M+1,\dots,100.

In particular, \(a_M=100\). For \(i<M\), the Lipschitz condition gives

\[
a_i\ge 100-(M-i)=100-M+i.

Also \(a_i\ge M\). Hence for \(i<M\),

and for \(i\ge M\),

\[
r=100-M.

Since the minimum value \(M\) must actually be attained before position \(M\), we need \(M\ge 51\), so

\[
0\le r\le 49.

Thus

\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i

\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i

for integer \(0\le r\le 49\). Its vertex is at

r=\frac{199}{6}\approx 33.17,

M=100-33=67.

10000-\frac{199\cdot 33-3\cdot 33^2}{2}

### 1. Basic consequences

If some value \(v\) appears among the \(a_i\), say \(v=a_i=f(i)\), then

\[
a_v=f(v)=f(f(i))=100.
\]

So every value that appears as an output must be an index where the function value is \(100\).

Also, since \(100=f(f(i))\) for every \(i\), the value \(100\) appears, and therefore

\[
a_{100}=100.
\]

Let

\[
m=\min_{1\le i\le 100} a_i.
\]

Since the sequence \((a_i)\) changes by at most \(1\) between neighboring indices and it contains both \(m\) and \(100\), it must take every integer value from \(m\) to \(100\). Hence every value \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) appears as an output, so

\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.
\]

Thus the suffix from index \(m\) onward is forced to be \(100\).

### 2. Lower bound for fixed \(m\)

For \(i<m\), since \(a_m=100\) and adjacent values differ by at most \(1\),

\[
a_i\ge 100-(m-i)=i+100-m.
\]

Also \(a_i\ge m\), by definition of \(m\). Therefore

\[
a_i\ge \max(m,i+100-m)\qquad (i<m).
\]

For \(m\) to actually be the minimum, we need \(m\ge 51\).

For fixed \(m\in\{51,\dots,99\}\), the smallest possible sequence is therefore

\[
a_i=
\begin{cases}
m, & 1\le i\le 2m-100,\\
i+100-m, & 2m-99\le i\le m-1,\\
100, & m\le i\le 100.
\end{cases}
\]

Its sum is

\[
S(m)=m(2m-100)+\sum_{k=m+1}^{99}k+100(101-m).
\]

Compute:

\[
S(m)=m(2m-100)+\frac{(m+1+99)(99-m)}2+100(101-m).
\]

Simplifying,

\[
S(m)=\frac{3m^2-401m+30100}{2}.
\]

This quadratic is minimized near

\[
m=\frac{401}{6}\approx 66.83,
\]

so we check \(m=67\):

\[
S(67)=\frac{3\cdot 67^2-401\cdot 67+30100}{2}=8350.
\]

### 3. Construction attaining the bound

Take

\[
f(i)=

67, & 1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33, & 35\le i\le 66,\\
100, & 67\le i\le 100.

Then adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), and the image is \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\), all of whose indices map to \(100\). Hence \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\).

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{8350}

\[
|a_i-a_{i+1}|\le 1,\qquad a_{a_i}=100.

If \(v\) is in the image of \(f\), then \(v=a_i\) for some \(i\), so

\[
a_v=f(v)=f(f(i))=100.

Thus every value attained by \(f\) is an index where \(f\) equals \(100\).

Let

\[
m=\min_{1\le i\le 100} a_i.

Since \(100\) is attained as a value, we have \(a_{100}=100\). Also, moving from an index where \(a_i=m\) to index \(100\), the sequence must pass through every value from \(m\) to \(100\). Hence every integer \(m,m+1,\dots,100\) is in the image, so

\[
a_m=a_{m+1}=\cdots=a_{100}=100.

For \(i<m\), we therefore have

\[
a_i\ge m

and also, by the Lipschitz condition from \(i\) to \(m\),

\[
a_i\ge 100-(m-i)=i+100-m.

So

\[
a_i\ge \max(m,i+100-m).
\]

One also gets \(m\ge 51\). For fixed \(m\), the minimum possible sum is therefore

\[
\sum_{i=1}^{100} a_i
\ge (2m-100)m+\sum_{v=m+1}^{99}v+100(101-m).
\]

For \(51\le m\le 99\), this simplifies to

\[

This quadratic is minimized at \(m=67\), giving

\[
\frac{3\cdot 67^2-401\cdot 67+30100}{2}=8350.

It remains to check attainability. Define

\[
f(i)=

67,&1\le i\le 34,\\
i+33,&35\le i\le 66,\\
100,&67\le i\le 100.

Then adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), the image is \(\{67,68,\dots,100\}\), and every element of the image maps to \(100\). Thus \(f(f(i))=100\) for all \(i\).

Therefore the minimum possible value is

\[
\boxed{8350}

Let

\[
m=\min_{1\le i\le 100} f(i).

We first extract some structural consequences.

Since \(f(f(i))=100\), every value taken by \(f\) is an index where \(f\) equals \(100\). In particular, if \(k\) appears as some \(f(i)\), then

\[
f(k)=100.
\]

Also, because some \(j\) has \(f(j)=100\), we get

\[
f(100)=f(f(j))=100.

Now suppose \(m<100\), and let \(r\) be such that \(f(r)=m\). Since \(f(100)=100\), and adjacent values differ by at most \(1\), the values along the path from \(r\) to \(100\) must pass through every integer from \(m\) to \(100\). Hence every integer \(k\in\{m,m+1,\dots,100\}\) is in the image of \(f\), so

\[
f(k)=100\qquad (m\le k\le 100).

\[
f(m)=100.

Since \(f(r)=m\) and \(f(m)=100\), the Lipschitz condition gives

\[
100-m\le |m-r|.

But \(r<m\), otherwise \(r\in\{m,\dots,100\}\) would imply \(f(r)=100\), contradicting \(f(r)=m<100\). Therefore \(|m-r|\le m-1\), so

\[
100-m\le m-1,

which implies

\[
m\ge 51.

Now for \(i<m\), using \(f(m)=100\),

\[
f(i)\ge 100-(m-i)=100-m+i.
\]

Also \(f(i)\ge m\), so

\[
f(i)\ge \max(m,100-m+i).
